Aleisha White, Esq. 408-286-5800 Barliner Cohen LLP for Defendant Flights Restaurant et al.
Court Reporter: Deanna Goyke, CSR #11413, 408-920-0222, dgoyke@comcats.net, reported.

RULING - A R G U E D. Ms. White argues defamation and negligence claims. Mr. Bollier agrees with the tentative ruling and responds to Ms. White's arguments. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentaitive ruling as follows:

DEFENDANTS FLIGHTS RESTAURANT BURLINGAME, INC., FLIGHTS RESTAURANT GROUP, INC., FLIGHTS RESTAURANT HOLDINGS, INC.'S Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings.

Flights Restaurant Burlingame, Inc., Flights Restaurant Group, Inc. and Flights Restaurant Holdings, Inc's (collectively Restaurant) judgment on the pleadings as to the first amended complaint (FAC) is denied in part and granted in part.

Richard Cibotti adequately pleads slander and false light against the Restaurant. The FAC alleges, inter alia, that Restaurant employees falsely "orally identif[ied]" Richard as the man who made "threats to kill a family and their young children" at the Restaurant. (Id. at 5:3-24.) Contrary to the Restaurant's argument, the FAC specifically pleads that the Restaurant's identification of Richard was "false." (See, e.g., FAC 5:21.) And a statement need not allege commission of a crime to be slanderous, though this one did. (CALCRIM 1300.)
As to false light, threatening to "kill a family" would expose one to contempt at a minimum.
Richard Cibotti adequately pleads libel against the Restaurant. The FAC alleges, inter alia, that the Restaurant produced a credit card receipt bearing Richard's name "in response to inquiries by" the threatened family "as to the identity of the individual who had threatened them." (Id. at 5:12-16.) This written response to the family's queries again falsely identified Richard as the threatening individual, the FAC alleges. (Id. at 5:3-24.)
The FAC adequately pleads its negligence theories. The Restaurant owed general duties to plaintiffs (Civ. Code 1714) as well as heightened duties from the restaurant-patron special relationship (Delgado v. Trax Bar & Grill (2005) 36 Cal.4th 224, 235).

Whether negligent infliction of emotional distress is mislabeled a cause of action is of no moment. Likewise, because a demurrer must entirely eliminate a count, Tiffani Cibotti is irrelevant here. (Elder v. Pacific Bell Telephone Co. (2012) 205 Cal.App.841, 856 n.14.) However, plaintiffs cite no authority for their notion that a restaurant has fiduciary duties to its patrons, so the motion is granted as to that cause of action without leave to amend. The motion is denied as to all other counts.

Order is signed.
